source_book: NCERT Class 12 Physics, Ch.3 Current Electricity (§3.13 Kirchhoff's Rules — the junction/current rule half) — table-of-contents + scope reference only; teaching authored from first principles. DC Pandey consulted for chapter SCOPE only (Current Electricity ToC places Kirchhoff's rules after combination_of_resistors/emf/internal_resistance/electrical_power, before Wheatstone/potentiometer) to confirm the atomic boundary — no teaching method, example problem, or figure imported. The atomic idea: at any junction (node) in a circuit, the total current flowing IN equals the total current flowing OUT — Σi_in = Σi_out — because charge is conserved and cannot pile up or vanish at a point; when current splits at a fork it redistributes by branch conductance (1/R), but the branch currents always add back to exactly what entered. Does NOT cover the loop rule / KVL (deferred to kirchhoff_loop_rule_KVL, a separate diamond), solving multi-loop networks (downstream application), the current-divider formula as a taught derivation (it emerges as a NUMBER on the ammeters here, never derived on canvas — that belongs to combination_of_resistors / a future drill-down), or Wheatstone/potentiometer (downstream catalog nodes that depend on this one). EIGHTH Ch.3 concept on the particle_field (2D p5.js) renderer, same engine family as drift_velocity/ohms_law/resistivity/combination_of_resistors/emf_definition/internal_resistance/electrical_power_in_resistor. Renderer upgrade (founder-directed, 2026-07-11): the particle_field circuit engine (`combination_of_resistors` scenario_type) was extended with the KCL flag set — a per-state R1 numeric lock (circuitMode's cR1() now honors state.R1 exactly like the pre-existing R2 lock), a genuine THIRD parallel branch (R3 + state.three_branch, feeding node N with a real 3-way split in circuitGeom()/cCurrents()), show_branch_meters + branch_labels for symbolic per-branch ammeters, show_in_meter/in_meter_label + main_meter_label (the A_in / A_out framing), kcl_sum_readout (an on-canvas value-only Σ HUD, auto-formatting 2- or 3-branch sums, and switching #pm-readout to 'A_in = A_out' so R_eq is suppressed on these states per the taught invariant), ghost_text/ghost_pos (a fixed, config-driven struck-through naive-expectation string), and node_label (labels junction N, reusing the existing `junction` glow key — no new primitive needed). This concept now renders the FULL physics-author-verified numbers with no adapted or workaround values: S1/S2 R1=R2=3Ω → I_total=2.0A, I1=I2=1.0A; S3 R1=6Ω,R2=2Ω (both dials move together from the S1/S2 baseline) → R_eq unchanged at 1.5Ω (Rule 32b's deliberate invariant — total pinned while only the split changes), I1=0.5A, I2=1.5A, sum still 2.0A; S4 ships as the 3-branch PRIMARY path (not the 2-branch fallback) — R1=2Ω, R2=10Ω, R3=15Ω → R_eq=1.5Ω a third time, I1=1.5A, I2=0.3A, I3=0.2A, summing to 2.0A. Conceptual-only (no mode_overrides), EPIC-L-first (no epic_c_branches) — the two genuine misconceptions ('current gets used up', 'a fork always splits fifty-fifty') are confronted inside EPIC-L via Rule 16a misconception_watch, both fired together at the STATE_3 pivot only (founder guardrail 2026-07-04: no per-state tic), no predict-pause. Anchors authored UNIVERSAL per Rule 35 (a wall socket feeding a power strip; a T-junction in a water pipe — no country, brand, festival, or currency; plain English throughout, no Hinglish).

real_world_anchor.primary: A wall socket feeding a power strip: the single supply current splits at the strip — some to each plugged-in device — and every branch current returns and sums back to exactly what the socket delivered, however unevenly it divided among the devices. Nothing is lost or stored at the split; the strip IS a junction.
real_world_anchor.secondary: Water flowing through a pipe that splits at a T-joint into two smaller pipes — the water doesn't compress or vanish at the joint, so however unevenly it divides between the two branches, the two flows always add back to what entered.

epsilon.name: Cell voltage (ideal, fixed)
epsilon.role: locked constant every state incl. explore; never a slider — set via particle_field_config.formula_anchor.constants.V_circuit (no V slider exists, so circuitMode's cVolt() falls through to this fixed value)

R1.name: Branch 1 resistance (top)
R1.role: per-state numeric lock (engine-verified: circuitMode's cR1() honors state.R1 identically to cR2raw()'s pre-existing R2 lock) — 3Ω on S1/S2, 6Ω on S3 (climbs together with R2's fall), 2Ω on S4 (3-branch primary); live-draggable in S3 (post-reveal) and S5 (explore)

R2.name: Branch 2 resistance (bottom)
R2.role: per-state numeric lock — 3Ω on S1/S2 (equal split), 2Ω on S3 (the CAUSE that moves first alongside R1, Rule 32a), 10Ω on S4 (3-branch primary); live-draggable in S3 (post-reveal) and S5 (explore)

R3.name: Branch 3 resistance (third parallel branch)
R3.role: only read when state.three_branch is true (S4, S5) — locked to 15Ω on S4 via state.R3, teacher-seizable; live-draggable only in S5 (explore)

R_eq.name: Equivalent resistance at N
R_eq.role: NOT displayed on canvas — KCL states switch #pm-readout to 'A_in = A_out' via kcl_sum_readout, suppressing R_eq; internal sanity value only — 1.5Ω identically on S2/S3/S4 by DELIBERATE authorial construction (Rule 32b: total pinned, only the split changes)

I_total.name: Total current (A_in = A_out)
I_total.role: live readout — the A_in ammeter (pre-fork) and the A_out ammeter (post-recombination) both read this identical number every instant, every state — this IS the KCL identity made visible with two instruments reading the same value

I1.name: Branch 1 current
I1.role: live readout — branch ammeter A₁: 1.0 A on S1/S2 (equal split), 0.5 A on S3 (R1 now the harder path), 1.5 A on S4 (R1=2Ω, now the easiest of three)

I2.name: Branch 2 current
I2.role: live readout — branch ammeter A₂: 1.0 A on S1/S2, 1.5 A on S3 (the easier 2Ω path), 0.3 A on S4 (R2=10Ω)

I3.name: Branch 3 current
I3.role: live readout — branch ammeter A₃; only rendered when state.three_branch is true — 0.2 A on S4 (R3=15Ω, the hardest of the three paths)

formulas.kcl_identity: I_total === I1 + I2 (+ I3) identically, by construction on this ideal-cell node — this IS the taught rule (Σi_in = Σi_out), not a coincidence to verify
formulas.current_divider_number_source: Ik = I_total*(R_eq/Rk) ≡ I_total*(1/Rk)/Σ(1/Rk) — NUMBER SOURCE ONLY for the ammeter readings; NEVER a taught derivation or on-canvas formula

constraints: I_total = I1 + I2 (+ I3) EXACTLY at every setting — this is the definition of the taught rule, true by the circuit's construction, not an approximation
the A_in ammeter (pre-fork) and A_out ammeter (post-recombination) read the SAME number at every instant, every state — the visual proof that current is never 'used up'
split ratio is set by CONDUCTANCE (1/R): the lower-resistance branch always carries proportionally more current, never an automatic 50/50 share regardless of the resistor values
R_eq = 1.5Ω identically on S2/S3/S4 — DELIBERATE authorial invariant (Rule 32b: A_in/A_out hold still while only the split changes) — do NOT 'fix'

R1/R2 both move together S1/S2 → S3 (3,3)→(6,2) to hold R_eq fixed while flipping the split; S4 adds a genuine THIRD parallel branch (R1=2,R2=10,R3=15) rendered natively in circuitGeom()/cCurrents() — no fallback 2-branch reframe needed
all currents ≥ 0; R1,R2,R3 ∈ [1,20]Ω so no divide-by-zero, no negative resistance, no infinite current
energy/heat is OUT OF SCOPE (that is electrical_power_in_resistor) — no P=I²R anywhere on canvas or in narration
the current-divider formula is a number-source only, engine-internal — never an on-canvas formula or a narrated derivation; only Σi_in = Σi_out is taught, first shown formally at STATE_4

aha_moment.statement: The split looks broken — yet total in still equals total out.

STATE_1.title: Charge can't pile up: beads stream through node N

scene_composition.text: One cell drives current into a single junction, node N.
Beads arrive and leave node N at exactly the same rate.
Charge cannot vanish or stack up at a single point in a wire.

tts_sentences.text_en: Current flows from the cell into a single junction — node N — and splits into two branches.
Watch node N closely: beads arrive and leave at exactly the same rate, so nothing ever piles up there.
Charge cannot vanish or pile up at a single point in a wire.

STATE_2.title: Splits, then adds back: equal branches, in = branch₁ + branch₂

scene_composition.text: The stream forks into two equal three-ohm branches at node N.
Branch ammeters settle at one ampere each, main ammeter two amperes.
Two amps in equals one plus one equals two amps out.

tts_sentences.text_en: The stream reaches node N and splits into two equal branches, three ohms each.
Watch the branch ammeters settle: one ampere down each path, while the main ammeter still reads two amperes total.
Two amps in equals one plus one equals two amps out — the split adds back to exactly what entered.

STATE_3.title: PRIMARY aha — unequal branches redistribute, sum still conserved

scene_composition.text: R one climbs to six ohms; R two falls to two ohms — R two is now the easier path.
Branch two climbs to one point five amperes; branch one falls to zero point five.
Not one plus one — the easier path takes three times as much: zero point five plus one point five.

tts_sentences.text_en: R one climbs to six ohms; R two falls to two — now the easier path.
The streams reshuffle: branch two climbs to one point five amperes, branch one falls to zero point five.
Not one plus one — the easier path takes three times as much, yet the total holds at two amperes.

misconception_watch.belief: Current gets used up as it flows through a resistor.
misconception_watch.visual_counter: The A_in and A_out ammeters both read 2.00 A — the same number whether read as what enters the split or what leaves after the branches recombine; the loop closes at full current every cycle.
misconception_watch.one_line_fix: Current is charge in motion — it is never used up, only energy is.

misconception_watch.belief: A fork always splits current fifty-fifty.
misconception_watch.visual_counter: Naively 2.00 A should split 1.00 + 1.00, but the two-ohm branch (the easier path) actually carries 1.50 A against the six-ohm branch's 0.50 A — still summing to exactly 2.00 A.
misconception_watch.one_line_fix: The easier path (lower R) takes more — but the parts always add back to the whole.

STATE_4.title: Generalization: ANY junction, any number of wires — Σi_in = Σi_out

scene_composition.text: A third branch joins the fork — the same balance holds no matter how many wires meet here.
One point five plus zero point three plus zero point two amperes in still equals two amperes out.
Sum of current in equals sum of current out — Kirchhoff's junction rule.

tts_sentences.text_en: A third branch joins the fork — three paths now leave node N.
Watch the three ammeters settle: one point five, zero point three, and zero point two amperes.
Add them up: one point five plus zero point three plus zero point two — still two amperes, in equals out at any junction.

STATE_5.title: Explore — every dial is yours: R1, R2, every ammeter live

scene_composition.text: Both resistors are live now — drag either one.
Every ammeter and the formula respond live as you drag.
The junction rule holds at every setting you choose.

tts_sentences.text_en: Every dial is yours now — drag either resistor and watch the junction rule hold at every setting.

assessment.mastery_definition: A student has mastered Kirchhoff's junction rule when they can (1) state that charge cannot accumulate or vanish at any point in a circuit, so the total current entering a junction must equal the total current leaving it, second for second; (2) predict that current splits UNEVENLY between unequal branches — by conductance (1/R), not automatically fifty-fifty — while the branch currents still sum to exactly what entered; (3) recognize that a resistor spends ENERGY (as heat), never CHARGE, so an ammeter reads the same current before a split and after the branches recombine; (4) apply Σi_in = Σi_out to solve for an unknown branch current at a junction with any number of wires; and (5) explain WHY the rule holds at every junction regardless of branch count — it follows directly from conservation of charge, not from a rule specific to two-way forks.

questions.stem: Electrons flow steadily into a junction in a wire and immediately split into two branches. What happens to the charge AT the junction itself, second after second?

options.A: None of it accumulates — exactly as much charge arrives each second as leaves, so the junction never gains or loses charge
options.B: A small amount accumulates at the junction, slowly charging it up over time
options.C: Charge is briefly stored and released in bursts, causing the current to pulse
options.D: The charge arriving is partly converted to heat right at the junction, so less charge leaves than arrives

distractor_misconceptions.B: imagines a junction as a leaky reservoir that could store charge, rather than a simple conductor with no capacity to accumulate it
distractor_misconceptions.C: invents a pulsing/bursting behavior with no physical mechanism — steady inflow gives steady, not pulsed, outflow
distractor_misconceptions.D: confuses charge (strictly conserved) with energy (which can convert to heat) — no charge is destroyed anywhere in a circuit

questions.tested_idea: Charge cannot accumulate or vanish at a junction — exactly as much flows out each second as flows in.
questions.parallel_form_stem: A junction has current flowing steadily into it from one wire and out through two others. Explain what this implies about the charge sitting AT the junction at any instant.

questions.stem: Two identical resistors branch off the same junction from a single incoming current of 2.0 A. How does the current divide?

options.A: 1.0 A down each branch — equal resistors share the current equally, and the two branches still add up to the full 2.0 A
options.B: 2.0 A down each branch — every branch always carries the full incoming current
options.C: The current chooses only one branch and ignores the other entirely
options.D: 1.0 A down each branch, but only 1.5 A total leaves the junction — the rest is lost in the split

distractor_misconceptions.B: treats every branch as carrying the FULL current independently, ignoring that current entering a junction is shared among the paths, not duplicated
distractor_misconceptions.C: assumes current is all-or-nothing at a fork, rather than dividing continuously across every available path
distractor_misconceptions.D: reintroduces the used-up misconception — the branch currents must still sum to exactly the incoming current, 2.0 A, with nothing lost

questions.tested_idea: When branch resistances are equal, current splits equally between them, and the branches still sum to the total that entered.
questions.parallel_form_stem: A junction with two identical branches has 6.0 A entering it. State how that current divides, and confirm the branches still add to 6.0 A.

questions.stem: At a junction with two very different branch resistances, the total current entering is 2.0 A. After it passes through both branches and recombines, what does the ammeter after the junction read?

options.A: 2.0 A — exactly the same as before the split; nothing was consumed by the resistors along the way
options.B: Less than 2.0 A, because the resistors used up some of the current
options.C: More than 2.0 A, because the resistors add extra current
options.D: It depends on which resistor is bigger — the reading changes with resistance

distractor_misconceptions.B: the classic 'current gets used up' trap — resistors dissipate ENERGY as heat, they do not consume CHARGE
distractor_misconceptions.C: invents current being created from nothing, violating charge conservation just as badly as consuming it would
distractor_misconceptions.D: confuses how the SPLIT changes with resistance (true) with the TOTAL changing after recombination (false) — total in must equal total out regardless of the split

questions.tested_idea: The current measured after a junction's branches recombine equals the current that entered — resistors spend energy, never charge.
questions.parallel_form_stem: A junction splits 5.0 A between two unequal branches that later recombine. State the ammeter reading after recombination, and justify it.

questions.stem: A junction splits into a 6 Ω branch and a 2 Ω branch, with 2.0 A entering total. How does the 2.0 A actually divide?

options.A: 0.5 A down the 6 Ω branch and 1.5 A down the 2 Ω branch — the lower-resistance path carries three times as much, not an equal share
options.B: 1.0 A down each branch, since a fork always splits current evenly
options.C: All 2.0 A goes down the 2 Ω branch, since current always avoids resistance entirely
options.D: 0.5 A down each branch, with the remaining 1.0 A lost at the junction

distractor_misconceptions.B: the fifty-fifty misconception — an unequal split by conductance is the norm, not a special case
distractor_misconceptions.C: overcorrects into all-or-nothing routing, ignoring that current flows down EVERY available path, just unevenly
distractor_misconceptions.D: reintroduces the used-up misconception on top of the wrong split ratio

questions.tested_idea: Current splits by conductance (1/R) — the lower-resistance branch carries proportionally more, never an automatic equal share.
questions.parallel_form_stem: A junction splits into a 2 Ω branch and a 6 Ω branch, with 4.0 A entering total. Find the current in each branch.

questions.stem: At a junction, currents of 3 A and 2 A flow IN along two wires, and 4 A flows OUT along a third wire. A fourth wire is also connected to this junction. What current flows in that fourth wire, and in which direction?

options.A: 1 A, flowing OUT — the total in (3+2=5 A) must equal the total out, so the remaining 1 A must also leave
options.B: 1 A, flowing IN — extra current always enters wherever there's room
options.C: 5 A, flowing OUT — all incoming current must double back out somewhere
options.D: 0 A — a junction can only have three wires, so the fourth carries nothing

distractor_misconceptions.B: gets the magnitude right but the direction backwards — since 5 A already entered and only 4 A left, the missing 1 A must be LEAVING, not entering, to keep the sums equal
distractor_misconceptions.C: confuses the total in (5 A) with what must additionally flow out, rather than subtracting what's already accounted for
distractor_misconceptions.D: invents a wire-count limit that doesn't exist — Kirchhoff's junction rule applies to ANY number of wires meeting at a point

questions.tested_idea: Σi_in = Σi_out holds at a junction with any number of wires — solve for the unknown branch by balancing the sums.
questions.parallel_form_stem: At a junction, 6 A and 1 A flow IN along two wires, and 5 A flows OUT along one wire. A fourth wire also meets there. Find its current and direction.

questions.stem: Why does Σi_in = Σi_out hold at a junction no matter how many wires meet there, or how the current happens to divide among them?

options.A: Because charge is conserved at every point in a circuit — a junction with any number of wires cannot store or create charge, so whatever arrives each second must leave each second
options.B: Because engineers design junctions to always balance current on purpose
options.C: Because the rule only actually works for junctions with exactly two branches; more wires need a different law
options.D: Because resistance at the junction forces the currents to average out

distractor_misconceptions.B: attributes a law of physics to deliberate engineering, rather than the underlying conservation of charge that makes it universally true
distractor_misconceptions.C: wrongly restricts the rule to two-branch junctions — the SAME charge-conservation argument applies to a junction with any number of wires
distractor_misconceptions.D: invents a resistance-averaging mechanism; the junction itself has no resistance and does nothing to the currents except let them meet

questions.tested_idea: Σi_in = Σi_out generalizes to any junction because it follows directly from conservation of charge, not from a rule specific to two branches.
questions.parallel_form_stem: Explain why Kirchhoff's junction rule works just as well at a five-wire junction as it does at a two-branch fork.

regeneration_variants.label: A T-junction in a water pipe — flow in equals flow out
regeneration_variants.physical_world: Water flowing through a pipe that splits at a T-joint into two smaller pipes — the water doesn't compress or vanish at the joint, so however it divides between the two branches, the two flows always add back to the amount that entered.
regeneration_variants.same_physics: Same conservation identity as Σi_in=Σi_out — a continuity statement for a conserved quantity moving through a network of paths, whether that quantity is charge or water volume.
regeneration_variants.scene_hint: Narration-only secondary anchor for STATE_4's generalization beat; nothing pipe-shaped is drawn on canvas.

regeneration_variants.label: A power strip splitting one supply current across several devices
regeneration_variants.physical_world: A single wall-socket current feeds a power strip; several devices plug into it and each draws its own share, but the current returning through the strip's own cord always sums back to exactly what the socket delivered.
regeneration_variants.same_physics: Same Σi_in=Σi_out identity — the strip is a junction; a device with lower effective resistance draws a bigger share, exactly like the sim's unequal branches.
regeneration_variants.scene_hint: Primary teaching anchor (real_world_anchor.primary) restated as a Type-B variant for STATE_1/STATE_2.
